Name: temporal_redundancy_spectral_optimization
Title: Exploiting Temporal Redundancy for Training-Free Spectral Optimization in Sequential Inference
Short Hypothesis: Temporal redundancy in sequential data (e.g., video frames, time-series) can be leveraged to dynamically adjust spectral stability metrics, enabling training-free latency optimization without sacrificing accuracy.
Related Work: Existing methods like Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation focus on spatial redundancy but ignore temporal redundancy. Training-free approaches like TeDiO (Temporal Diagonal Optimization) address temporal coherence in video diffusion but do not optimize latency. Our proposal bridges this gap by explicitly exploiting temporal redundancy for spectral optimization.
Abstract: Sequential data, such as video frames or time-series, often exhibit significant temporal redundancy, where consecutive frames share similar features. We hypothesize that this redundancy can be leveraged to dynamically adjust spectral stability metrics during inference, enabling training-free latency optimization. We propose a framework that computes spectral stability metrics (e.g., condition number, spectral decay rate) for each frame and uses temporal consistency to predict stability for future frames. By skipping redundant computations in temporally stable regions, our method achieves significant latency reductions without sacrificing accuracy. We evaluate our approach on video diffusion models and time-series forecasting tasks, demonstrating superior throughput-accuracy trade-offs compared to static and heuristic baselines. Our theoretical analysis links temporal redundancy to PAC-Bayes generalization bounds, providing a mathematically transparent pathway for resource-efficient sequential inference.
Experiments: Video Diffusion Models: Apply our method to Stable Diffusion for video generation. Measure latency, throughput, and visual quality (CLIP score, FID) under varying temporal redundancy thresholds.
Time-Series Forecasting: Test on time-series datasets (e.g., ETTh1, Traffic). Evaluate latency, prediction accuracy (MAE, RMSE), and spectral stability metrics.
Ablation Study: Vary the temporal redundancy threshold and spectral stability metrics to analyze their impact on latency and accuracy.
Comparison to Baselines: Compare against static spectral optimization (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing) and heuristic temporal redundancy methods (e.g., frame skipping).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Degraded Performance: Excessive skipping of redundant computations may lead to accuracy loss in highly dynamic regions.
Overhead: Computing spectral stability metrics for each frame may introduce additional latency.
Generalization: The method may not generalize well to sequences with irregular temporal patterns.
